{"content": "# SETTLEMENT STATEMENT — NOTES MEMO

## Discrepancies, Assumptions, and Items Requiring Confirmation

**Transaction:** Sale of 4280 Harborview Boulevard, Bridgeport, CT 06604
**Closing Date:** July 15, 2025
**Seller:** Estate of Gerald T. Whitford (Claudia Whitford-Barnes, Executrix)
**Buyer:** Meridian Cove Properties LLC (Elaine R. Matsuda, Manager)
**Prepared by:** Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC, Closing Agent

**Re:** Settlement Statement dated July 15, 2025

---

**Purpose.** This memorandum accompanies the settlement statement prepared for the above-referenced closing. It identifies (A) discrepancies among the closing documents, (B) assumptions made in preparing the settlement statement where the record is silent or ambiguous, and (C) items requiring confirmation or post-closing action. Each item is keyed to the source document and, where applicable, to the affected settlement-statement line. The settlement statement should be read together with this memo.

---

## A. DISCREPANCIES AMONG CLOSING DOCUMENTS

### A-1. Title commitment number mismatch
The Purchase & Sale Agreement (Section 5.1) references the title commitment as "Commitment No. PCT-2025-08814," dated April 30, 2025. The actual title commitment delivered by Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC bears Commitment No. **TC-2025-07182**, effective June 18, 2025. The commitment number and effective date in the PSA do not match the commitment in the closing file. The settlement statement uses the actual commitment (TC-2025-07182). **Action:** Confirm with the Title Company that TC-2025-07182 is the operative commitment and that no separate commitment PCT-2025-08814 exists or was superseded.

### A-2. Title insurer name inconsistency within the title commitment
The title commitment header states it is issued "on behalf of **Continental Fiducial Title Insurance Company**," but the body and Conditions & Stipulations refer to "**Continental Hartleigh Title Insurance Company**" as "the Company." The countersignature block again references "Continental Fiducial Title Insurance Company." It is unclear whether the underwriter is Continental Fiducity or Continental Hartleigh. **Action:** Confirm the correct legal name of the title insurer for the owner's and lender's policies; correct the commitment and policy forms as needed.

### A-3. Recording references for liens differ between title commitment and payoff letters
The recording references (volume/page) for the existing liens are inconsistent between the title commitment (Schedule B-I) and the lien payoff letter compilation prepared by Seller's counsel:

| Lien | Title Commitment Reference | Payoff Letter / Summary Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Harborstone 1st Mortgage | Vol. 1087, Pg. 445 | Vol. 312, Pg. 88 *(this is the property plat/deed reference, not the mortgage)* |
| Harborstone HELOC | Vol. 1204, Pg. 218 | Vol. 487, Pg. 112 |
| Northbridge Mechanic's Lien | Vol. 1398, Pg. 77 | Vol. 1064, Pg. 517 |
| CT DRS Estate Tax Lien | *(not specified)* | Vol. 1087, Pg. 234 |

The payoff letter for the first mortgage incorrectly cites "Volume 312, Page 88" as the mortgage recording reference — that volume/page is the Bridgeport Harbor Redevelopment Plat (the property's legal description), not the mortgage recording. **Action:** Reconcile the correct recording references with the Bridgeport Land Records before recording releases; ensure each release instrument cites the correct volume/page of the lien it discharges. The settlement statement's payoff amounts are unaffected, but the release instruments must reference the correct recordings.

### A-4. Utility bill treatment — PSA vs. municipal lien certificate
The PSA (Section 7.5(a)) directs that where a utility billing period ends on or before the Closing Date, "the full unpaid balance shall be credited to Buyer on the settlement statement." The water/sewer billing period (May 15 – July 14, 2025) ends July 14, one day before closing, so the entire $1,847.60 is Seller's responsibility. However, both the utility account statement and the municipal tax status certificate (No. TLC-2025-04892) state that this balance "must be satisfied at or before closing," even though the bill is not due until August 1, 2025. The settlement statement follows the PSA (debit Seller / credit Buyer, Lines 508/204). **Action:** The Closing Agent should confirm whether the WPCA bill is paid directly from Seller's proceeds at closing (satisfying the municipal lien certificate) or credited to Buyer for post-closing payment. If paid directly from Seller's proceeds, the credit to Buyer (Line 204) should be removed and Buyer's cash adjusted upward by $1,847.60; the reconciliation remains balanced either way.

### A-5. First mortgage per diem interest rate appears inconsistent with stated rate
The first mortgage payoff statement shows accrued interest of $73.07/day for the 12-day period (consistent with 3.875% on the ~$685,921 balance: $685,921 × 3.875% ÷ 365 ≈ $72.83/day). However, the stated per diem after the good-through date is **$112.67/day**, which implies an effective rate of approximately 5.99% — inconsistent with the stated 3.875% fixed rate. The payoff letter attributes this to "the higher daily interest accrual rate that applies to the declining principal balance," which does not explain a higher (not lower) per diem. **Impact on settlement:** None, because closing (July 15) falls within the good-through period (through July 20) and no per diem adjustment is required. **Action:** If closing is delayed beyond July 20, 2025, obtain a corrected/updated payoff statement and verify the per diem before disbursing additional interest.

### A-6. Address inconsistencies for Closing Agent and Buyer's counsel
The closing documents contain multiple, inconsistent addresses for the same parties:

| Party | PSA / Title Commitment | Lien Payoff Letter | Tax Lien Certificate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC | 88 Field Point Road, Greenwich, CT 06830 | 3100 Main Street, Suite 204, Bridgeport, CT 06604 | 1200 Post Road East, Suite 214, Westport, CT 06880 |
| Ridgeline Law Group PLLC (Buyer's Counsel) | 915 Chapel Street, Suite 400, New Haven, CT 06510 | 55 Railroad Avenue, Suite 300, Greenwich, CT 06830 | 915 Chapel Street, Suite 400, New Haven, CT 06510 |

**Action:** Confirm the correct mailing/service addresses for the Closing Agent and Buyer's counsel for purposes of wire instructions, document delivery, and post-closing correspondence. Wire instructions must be verified telephonically with a known contact regardless of the address listed.

### A-7. Escrow shortage included in first mortgage payoff
The Harborstone first mortgage payoff of $687,412.33 includes a $539.00 "Escrow Shortage / Deficiency" and a $75.00 "Release / Reconveyance Preparation Fee" in addition to the unpaid principal balance ($685,921.44) and accrued interest ($876.89). These are included in the total payoff disbursed from Seller's proceeds (Line 501). The $75.00 release preparation fee is distinct from the $73.00 recording fee for the release (Line 511). **Note:** Confirm with Seller's counsel that the escrow shortage is properly the Estate's obligation and that no escrow refund will be due to the Estate post-payoff (the payoff letter states overpayments are refunded to the Estate).

---

## B. ASSUMPTIONS MADE IN PREPARING THE SETTLEMENT STATEMENT

### B-1. Accrued interest on earnest money — not quantified (Lines 200a / 200a)
The PSA (Sections 3.2(d), 3.2(e)) requires the Earnest Money ($195,000.00) to be held in an interest-bearing account, with all accrued interest credited to Buyer at closing and applied against the Purchase Price. No document in the closing file states the interest amount earned on the First Deposit (held since April 24, 2025) or the Second Deposit (held since May 22, 2025). **Assumption:** The settlement statement credits $195,000.00 as earnest money with $0.00 accrued interest (Line 200a). When the Escrow Agent confirms the accrued interest, it shall be added as a credit to Buyer (Line 200a) and a corresponding debit to Seller, reducing Buyer's cash at closing and Seller's net proceeds by equal amounts.

### B-2. Accrued interest on residential security deposits — not quantified (Lines 203a / 507a)
The PSA (Section 7.4(a)) requires transfer of security deposits "together with any interest accrued thereon as required by applicable law." Connecticut General Statutes § 47a-21 requires interest on residential security deposits. The rent roll/security deposit schedule confirms residential deposits are held in an interest-bearing escrow account at Harborstone Federal Credit Union, but states no interest amount. The commercial deposit ($14,400.00) is held in a non-interest-bearing account per the management agreement (Section 6.2) and the commercial lease, so no interest accrues on it. **Assumption:** The settlement statement credits $32,400.00 in deposit principal only (Line 203), with $0.00 interest (Line 203a). When the Property Manager (Bayshore Management Co.) confirms the accrued interest on the 11 residential deposits, it shall be added as a credit to Buyer (Line 203a) and debit to Seller (Line 507a).

### B-3. Connecticut conveyance tax — estimated (Lines 512 / 900)
The total conveyance tax of $44,750.00 ($22,375.00 per party) is an estimate taken from the title commitment (Schedule C), stated to be "calculated using the tiered rate structure applicable to the City of Bridgeport." The PSA (Article 12) provides that the tax be "computed at the rate or rates applicable to the municipality" and split 50/50. The actual amount will be determined upon preparation and filing of Form OP-236 by the Closing Agent. **Assumption:** The settlement statement uses the title commitment's estimate. If the actual tax differs, each party's 50% share is adjusted equally; the net effect on the reconciliation is zero (both sides move by the same amount).

### B-4. FY 2025–2026 property tax — estimated using current year (Lines 205 / 509)
The municipal tax status certificate (Section 3) confirms that tax bills for the fiscal year commencing July 1, 2025 have not been issued and the new mill rate has not been set. Per PSA Section 7.1(b), the parties agreed to prorate using the current year's tax of $52,480.00 as the estimate. **Assumption:** The settlement statement uses $52,480.00 ÷ 365 = $143.7808/day, with Seller charged for 14 days ($2,012.93) and Buyer responsible for 351 days ($50,467.07). The parties shall reprorate within 90 days after closing when the actual bill is issued (PSA Section 7.7).

### B-5. Rent proration methodology — per-lease aggregation (Lines 202 / 506)
The PSA (Section 7.2(d)) requires rents to be "calculated separately for each lease and then aggregated into a single net proration amount." When calculated per-lease and aggregated, Seller's share is $10,658.05 and Buyer's share is $12,941.95. When calculated on the aggregate collected rent ($23,600.00 × 14/31 and × 17/31), Seller's share would be $10,658.06 and Buyer's share $12,941.94 — a $0.01 difference due to rounding. **Assumption:** The settlement statement uses the per-lease method as required by the PSA, yielding Buyer's share of $12,941.95. The $0.01 difference is allocated to Buyer, consistent with the PSA's rounding rule (Section 7.2(c): "any remaining balance allocated to Buyer").

### B-6. No brokerage commission
Per PSA Section 15.7, no broker, finder, or real estate agent was involved and no commission is due. **Assumption:** No brokerage commission line item appears on the settlement statement. Each party indemnifies the other against any brokerage claim.

### B-7. No insurance proration
The PSA does not provide for proration of property/casualty insurance. The Seller's property insurance terminates at closing; the Buyer is required to obtain a new policy (Lender Commitment, Section 5.5: minimum $3,900,000 replacement cost, Tidewater Savings Bank named as mortgagee/loss payee, carrier rated A- or better). **Assumption:** No insurance premium or proration appears on the settlement statement. Confirm Buyer's evidence of insurance is delivered at closing as a loan condition.

### B-8. Survey, environmental, and SNDA costs not specified
The title commitment (Requirement 12) requires an ALTA/NSPS survey; the lender (Section 5.4) requires a survey and flood determination; the lender (Section 5.6) requires a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment; and the lender (Section 5.7) requires a Subordination, Non-Disturbance, and Attornment Agreement (SNDA) with Coastal Provisions Market LLC. None of these costs is stated in the PSA, title commitment, or lender commitment as a closing-table charge. **Assumption:** These items are either already completed or paid outside of closing (POC) by Buyer. No line item appears for them. Confirm with Buyer's counsel whether any survey, environmental, or SNDA fees are due at closing.

### B-9. Assignment of Leases and Rents to Lender — possible additional recording fee
The PSA (Section 6.4) lists three Buyer recording fees totaling $339.00: deed ($113), mortgage ($113), and assignment of leases ($113). The lender's recording instructions (Commitment Section 6.4) require recording of four instruments, including an "Assignment of Leases and Rents from Meridian Cove Properties LLC to Tidewater Savings Bank" (item 4). It is unclear whether the $113 "Assignment of Leases" recording fee in the PSA covers the Seller-to-Buyer assignment, the Buyer-to-Lender assignment, or both. **Assumption:** The settlement statement includes one $113.00 assignment-of-leases recording fee (Line 800). If a separate recording fee is required for the Assignment of Leases and Rents to the Lender, an additional $113.00 Buyer charge must be added. Confirm with the Closing Agent and Lender.

### B-10. Utility deposits retained by Seller
Per PSA Section 7.5(d), utility deposits held by utility providers in Seller's name are retained by Seller unless the parties otherwise agree. **Assumption:** No utility deposit transfer or credit appears on the settlement statement. Buyer is responsible for establishing new utility accounts as of the Closing Date.

---

## C. ITEMS REQUIRING CONFIRMATION OR POST-CLOSING ACTION

### C-1. Lien payoff good-through dates — closing must occur by July 20, 2025
The Harborstone first mortgage ($687,412.33) and HELOC ($148,219.56) payoffs are good through July 20, 2025. The municipal tax payoff is also valid through July 20, 2025. The scheduled closing date (July 15, 2025) falls within all good-through periods, so no per diem adjustments are required. **If closing is delayed beyond July 20, 2025:** updated payoff statements must be obtained and per diem interest added — 1st Mortgage: $112.67/day; HELOC: ~$27.93/day (variable, subject to Prime Rate change); delinquent tax: $13.12/day. The Northbridge mechanic's lien settlement ($31,000.00) is contingent on closing by August 31, 2025, after which Northbridge may withdraw the settlement and pursue the full $37,500.00 lien.

### C-2. CT DRS estate tax lien release — recording required
No monetary payoff is due ($0.00). The estate is below the Connecticut estate tax threshold. A release (CT DRS Release Certificate No. ETL-2025-08834) has been obtained and must be recorded in the Bridgeport Land Records at Seller's expense ($73.00 recording fee included in Line 511). The title company (Requirement 7) and lender (Section 5.2(d)) both require this recording as a condition of insuring/funding. Confirm the release is delivered in recordable form and recorded.

### C-3. Fiduciary certificate recency
The Fiduciary's Certificate is dated June 25, 2025. The title commitment (Requirement 2) requires it dated no more than 60 days prior to closing; the lender (Section 5.12) requires it dated no earlier than 60 days prior to closing. July 15, 2025 is 20 days after June 25, 2025 — both requirements are satisfied. No action required, but confirm a certified copy is delivered and recorded.

### C-4. Probate Court Certificate — $150.00 Seller expense
The $150.00 Probate Court Certificate fee is a Seller expense (PSA Sections 6.2(e), 6.4). The fiduciary certificate document confirms the $150.00 certification fee has been paid. Reflected as Line 514 (debit to Seller).

### C-5. Management agreement termination — $4,500.00 Seller expense
The $4,500.00 termination fee is owed to Bayshore Management Co. per Section 11.2 of the management agreement (terminated effective July 15, 2025 per notice dated June 13, 2025), payable from sale proceeds at closing. Reflected as Line 515 (debit to Seller). Bayshore is directed to transfer all security deposits (with accrued residential interest) and deliver all records/keys to Buyer or the Closing Agent on or before the Closing Date.

### C-6. Repair escrow — separate agreement; lender is interested party
The $45,000.00 Repair Escrow (Line 516) is governed by a separate Repair Escrow Agreement to be executed at closing. The lender (Commitment Section 5.13) requires that (a) the Repair Escrow Agreement be provided for lender review prior to closing, (b) Tidewater Savings Bank be named as an interested party, and (c) disbursements occur only with the lender's prior written consent after satisfactory evidence of completed work. The pre-closing inspection report (July 14, 2025) confirms the roof deficiencies remain unrepaired and recommends Buyer obtain competitive bids promptly after closing. Confirm the Repair Escrow Agreement is executed and the lender's requirements are satisfied.

### C-7. Unit 2E delinquent rent — excluded; Buyer assumes collection right
Unit 2E's July 2025 rent ($1,600.00) is delinquent and excluded from the rent proration per PSA Section 7.3. Buyer assumes the right (but not the obligation) to collect delinquent rents post-closing. Any amounts collected by Buyer are first applied to current rents owed to Buyer; excess is remitted to Seller within 30 days (Section 7.3(b)). Seller may not commence or continue eviction/collection proceedings after closing without Buyer's consent (Section 7.3(c)).

### C-8. Month-to-month holdover tenancies assigned to Buyer
Six residential leases have expired and are now month-to-month holdover tenancies: Units 2B (expired 2/28/2025), 2C (expired 5/31/2025), 2E (expired 3/31/2025), 3B (expired 1/31/2025), 3D (expired 6/30/2025), and 3F (expired 4/30/2025). These are assigned to Buyer subject to the Permitted Exceptions (PSA Section 5.2(b)). Buyer assumes all landlord obligations for these holdover tenancies, including any risk of termination on proper notice. Buyer should review holdover provisions and consider lease renewals post-closing.

### C-9. Commercial lease — SNDA and assignment required
The Commercial Lease with Coastal Provisions Market LLC (10-year term, 1/1/2021–12/31/2030; monthly base rent $7,200; security deposit $14,400; NNN) is assigned to Buyer at closing. The lender requires an SNDA among the lender, Buyer, and Coastal Provisions Market LLC prior to or at closing (Commitment Section 5.7). The lease contains a 3% annual rent escalation (next increase 1/1/2026), two 5-year renewal options, and a right of first refusal on adjacent space. Confirm the SNDA is executed and the assignment is delivered in recordable form.

### C-10. Tenant notification within 15 days
Per PSA Section 11.3, Buyer must send written notice to all tenants within 15 days after closing advising of the change in ownership, new rent payment instructions, and the new holder of security deposits, in compliance with applicable Connecticut law.

### C-11. Personal guaranty required by lender
The lender (Commitment Section 5.10) requires Elaine R. Matsuda to execute an unconditional personal guaranty of payment and performance. This is a loan condition, not a settlement-statement item, but is noted for completeness. Confirm the guaranty is executed at closing.

### C-12. Closing protection letter and gap indemnity
The title commitment (Requirement 14) requires issuance of a closing protection letter in favor of Buyer and the Lender, and execution of a gap indemnity agreement covering the period from the title search effective date (June 16, 2025) through recording. Confirm these are delivered. No cost is stated in the closing file.

### C-13. Post-closing recording sequence
Per the lender's instructions (Commitment Section 6.4), documents must be recorded in the Bridgeport Land Records in this order: (1) releases/satisfactions of all existing liens; (2) Warranty Deed (Estate to Buyer); (3) Mortgage (Buyer to Lender); (4) Assignment of Leases and Rents (Buyer to Lender). This sequence preserves the lender's first-priority lien position. The Closing Agent must confirm releases are recorded or submitted for recording immediately following closing, with written confirmation to the lender within 60 days.

### C-14. Reproration right survives 90 days
All proration and adjustment obligations survive closing for 90 days (PSA Section 15.1) for purposes of reproration and final settlement (PSA Section 7.7). Any amounts owed upon reproration must be paid within 10 business days of demand with supporting documentation. Estimated items subject to reproration: FY 2025–2026 property taxes (B-4), and any utility charges if final readings were unavailable.

---

## SUMMARY OF FINANCIAL IMPACT OF UNCONFIRMED ITEMS

| Item | Line(s) | Current Treatment | Potential Adjustment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Earnest money interest | 200a / (Seller) | $0.00 | Credit to Buyer / Debit to Seller (reduces Buyer cash & Seller proceeds equally) |
| Security deposit interest | 203a / 507a | $0.00 | Credit to Buyer / Debit to Seller (reduces Buyer cash & Seller proceeds equally) |
| Conveyance tax (actual vs. est.) | 512 / 900 | $22,375.00 each | Adjusted equally on both sides; net reconciliation impact $0.00 |
| FY 2025–26 tax (actual vs. est.) | 205 / 509 | $2,012.93 Seller | Reprorated within 90 days post-closing |
| Assignment-to-Lender recording fee | 800 | $0.00 (not included) | Possible additional $113.00 Buyer charge |
| Utility bill paid at closing vs. credited | 204 / 508 | Credit to Buyer $1,847.60 | If paid directly, remove Buyer credit; add to Seller disbursement |

> **Net effect on reconciliation:** Items that adjust both Buyer and Seller equally (earnest money interest, security deposit interest, conveyance tax) have zero net impact on the reconciliation. Items that affect only one side (assignment-to-lender recording fee, utility treatment) require a corresponding adjustment to maintain the reconciliation. The settlement statement as prepared reconciles to $0.00 with the assumptions stated above.

---

*This Notes Memo is prepared by the Closing Agent for the benefit of the parties and their counsel and is intended to be read together with the settlement statement dated July 15, 2025. It does not constitute legal advice. Parties should review each item with their respective counsel prior to closing.*", "file_path": "settlement-statement-notes.md"}

{"content": "# SETTLEMENT STATEMENT — NOTES MEMO

## Discrepancies, Assumptions, and Items Requiring Confirmation

**Transaction:** Sale of 4280 Harborview Boulevard, Bridgeport, CT 06604
**Closing Date:** July 15, 2025
**Seller:** Estate of Gerald T. Whitford (Claudia Whitford-Barnes, Executrix)
**Buyer:** Meridian Cove Properties LLC (Elaine R. Matsuda, Manager)
**Prepared by:** Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC, Closing Agent

**Re:** Settlement Statement dated July 15, 2025

---

**Purpose.** This memorandum accompanies the settlement statement prepared for the above-referenced closing. It identifies (A) discrepancies among the closing documents, (B) assumptions made in preparing the settlement statement where the record is silent or ambiguous, and (C) items requiring confirmation or post-closing action. Each item is keyed to the source document and, where applicable, to the affected settlement-statement line. The settlement statement should be read together with this memo.

---

## A. DISCREPANCIES AMONG CLOSING DOCUMENTS

### A-1. Title commitment number mismatch
The Purchase & Sale Agreement (Section 5.1) references the title commitment as "Commitment No. PCT-2025-08814," dated April 30, 2025. The actual title commitment delivered by Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC bears Commitment No. **TC-2025-07182**, effective June 18, 2025. The commitment number and effective date in the PSA do not match the commitment in the closing file. The settlement statement uses the actual commitment (TC-2025-07182). **Action:** Confirm with the Title Company that TC-2025-07182 is the operative commitment and that no separate commitment PCT-2025-08814 exists or was superseded.

### A-2. Title insurer name inconsistency within the title commitment
The title commitment header states it is issued "on behalf of **Continental Fiducial Title Insurance Company**," but the body and Conditions & Stipulations refer to "**Continental Hartleigh Title Insurance Company**" as "the Company." The countersignature block again references "Continental Fiducial Title Insurance Company." It is unclear whether the underwriter is Continental Fiducity or Continental Hartleigh. **Action:** Confirm the correct legal name of the title insurer for the owner's and lender's policies; correct the commitment and policy forms as needed.

### A-3. Recording references for liens differ between title commitment and payoff letters
The recording references (volume/page) for the existing liens are inconsistent between the title commitment (Schedule B-I) and the lien payoff letter compilation prepared by Seller's counsel:

| Lien | Title Commitment Reference | Payoff Letter / Summary Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Harborstone 1st Mortgage | Vol. 1087, Pg. 445 | Vol. 312, Pg. 88 *(this is the property plat/deed reference, not the mortgage)* |
| Harborstone HELOC | Vol. 1204, Pg. 218 | Vol. 487, Pg. 112 |
| Northbridge Mechanic's Lien | Vol. 1398, Pg. 77 | Vol. 1064, Pg. 517 |
| CT DRS Estate Tax Lien | *(not specified)* | Vol. 1087, Pg. 234 |

The payoff letter for the first mortgage incorrectly cites "Volume 312, Page 88" as the mortgage recording reference — that volume/page is the Bridgeport Harbor Redevelopment Plat (the property's legal description), not the mortgage recording. **Action:** Reconcile the correct recording references with the Bridgeport Land Records before recording releases; ensure each release instrument cites the correct volume/page of the lien it discharges. The settlement statement's payoff amounts are unaffected, but the release instruments must reference the correct recordings.

### A-4. Utility bill treatment — PSA vs. municipal lien certificate
The PSA (Section 7.5(a)) directs that where a utility billing period ends on or before the Closing Date, "the full unpaid balance shall be credited to Buyer on the settlement statement." The water/sewer billing period (May 15 – July 14, 2025) ends July 14, one day before closing, so the entire $1,847.60 is Seller's responsibility. However, both the utility account statement and the municipal tax status certificate (No. TLC-2025-04892) state that this balance "must be satisfied at or before closing," even though the bill is not due until August 1, 2025. The settlement statement follows the PSA (debit Seller / credit Buyer, Lines 508/204). **Action:** The Closing Agent should confirm whether the WPCA bill is paid directly from Seller's proceeds at closing (satisfying the municipal lien certificate) or credited to Buyer for post-closing payment. If paid directly from Seller's proceeds, the credit to Buyer (Line 204) should be removed and Buyer's cash adjusted upward by $1,847.60; the reconciliation remains balanced either way.

### A-5. First mortgage per diem interest rate appears inconsistent with stated rate
The first mortgage payoff statement shows accrued interest of $73.07/day for the 12-day period (consistent with 3.875% on the ~$685,921 balance: $685,921 × 3.875% ÷ 365 ≈ $72.83/day). However, the stated per diem after the good-through date is **$112.67/day**, which implies an effective rate of approximately 5.99% — inconsistent with the stated 3.875% fixed rate. The payoff letter attributes this to "the higher daily interest accrual rate that applies to the declining principal balance," which does not explain a higher (not lower) per diem. **Impact on settlement:** None, because closing (July 15) falls within the good-through period (through July 20) and no per diem adjustment is required. **Action:** If closing is delayed beyond July 20, 2025, obtain a corrected/updated payoff statement and verify the per diem before disbursing additional interest.

### A-6. Address inconsistencies for Closing Agent and Buyer's counsel
The closing documents contain multiple, inconsistent addresses for the same parties:

| Party | PSA / Title Commitment | Lien Payoff Letter | Tax Lien Certificate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC | 88 Field Point Road, Greenwich, CT 06830 | 3100 Main Street, Suite 204, Bridgeport, CT 06604 | 1200 Post Road East, Suite 214, Westport, CT 06880 |
| Ridgeline Law Group PLLC (Buyer's Counsel) | 915 Chapel Street, Suite 400, New Haven, CT 06510 | 55 Railroad Avenue, Suite 300, Greenwich, CT 06830 | 915 Chapel Street, Suite 400, New Haven, CT 06510 |

**Action:** Confirm the correct mailing/service addresses for the Closing Agent and Buyer's counsel for purposes of wire instructions, document delivery, and post-closing correspondence. Wire instructions must be verified telephonically with a known contact regardless of the address listed.

### A-7. Escrow shortage included in first mortgage payoff
The Harborstone first mortgage payoff of $687,412.33 includes a $539.00 "Escrow Shortage / Deficiency" and a $75.00 "Release / Reconveyance Preparation Fee" in addition to the unpaid principal balance ($685,921.44) and accrued interest ($876.89). These are included in the total payoff disbursed from Seller's proceeds (Line 501). The $75.00 release preparation fee is distinct from the $73.00 recording fee for the release (Line 511). **Note:** Confirm with Seller's counsel that the escrow shortage is properly the Estate's obligation and that no escrow refund will be due to the Estate post-payoff (the payoff letter states overpayments are refunded to the Estate).

---

## B. ASSUMPTIONS MADE IN PREPARING THE SETTLEMENT STATEMENT

### B-1. Accrued interest on earnest money — not quantified (Lines 200a / 200a)
The PSA (Sections 3.2(d), 3.2(e)) requires the Earnest Money ($195,000.00) to be held in an interest-bearing account, with all accrued interest credited to Buyer at closing and applied against the Purchase Price. No document in the closing file states the interest amount earned on the First Deposit (held since April 24, 2025) or the Second Deposit (held since May 22, 2025). **Assumption:** The settlement statement credits $195,000.00 as earnest money with $0.00 accrued interest (Line 200a). When the Escrow Agent confirms the accrued interest, it shall be added as a credit to Buyer (Line 200a) and a corresponding debit to Seller, reducing Buyer's cash at closing and Seller's net proceeds by equal amounts.

### B-2. Accrued interest on residential security deposits — not quantified (Lines 203a / 507a)
The PSA (Section 7.4(a)) requires transfer of security deposits "together with any interest accrued thereon as required by applicable law." Connecticut General Statutes § 47a-21 requires interest on residential security deposits. The rent roll/security deposit schedule confirms residential deposits are held in an interest-bearing escrow account at Harborstone Federal Credit Union, but states no interest amount. The commercial deposit ($14,400.00) is held in a non-interest-bearing account per the management agreement (Section 6.2) and the commercial lease, so no interest accrues on it. **Assumption:** The settlement statement credits $32,400.00 in deposit principal only (Line 203), with $0.00 interest (Line 203a). When the Property Manager (Bayshore Management Co.) confirms the accrued interest on the 11 residential deposits, it shall be added as a credit to Buyer (Line 203a) and debit to Seller (Line 507a).

### B-3. Connecticut conveyance tax — estimated (Lines 512 / 900)
The total conveyance tax of $44,750.00 ($22,375.00 per party) is an estimate taken from the title commitment (Schedule C), stated to be "calculated using the tiered rate structure applicable to the City of Bridgeport." The PSA (Article 12) provides that the tax be "computed at the rate or rates applicable to the municipality" and split 50/50. The actual amount will be determined upon preparation and filing of Form OP-236 by the Closing Agent. **Assumption:** The settlement statement uses the title commitment's estimate. If the actual tax differs, each party's 50% share is adjusted equally; the net effect on the reconciliation is zero (both sides move by the same amount).

### B-4. FY 2025–2026 property tax — estimated using current year (Lines 205 / 509)
The municipal tax status certificate (Section 3) confirms that tax bills for the fiscal year commencing July 1, 2025 have not been issued and the new mill rate has not been set. Per PSA Section 7.1(b), the parties agreed to prorate using the current year's tax of $52,480.00 as the estimate. **Assumption:** The settlement statement uses $52,480.00 ÷ 365 = $143.7808/day, with Seller charged for 14 days ($2,012.93) and Buyer responsible for 351 days ($50,467.07). The parties shall reprorate within 90 days after closing when the actual bill is issued (PSA Section 7.7).

### B-5. Rent proration methodology — per-lease aggregation (Lines 202 / 506)
The PSA (Section 7.2(d)) requires rents to be "calculated separately for each lease and then aggregated into a single net proration amount." When calculated per-lease and aggregated, Seller's share is $10,658.05 and Buyer's share is $12,941.95. When calculated on the aggregate collected rent ($23,600.00 × 14/31 and × 17/31), Seller's share would be $10,658.06 and Buyer's share $12,941.94 — a $0.01 difference due to rounding. **Assumption:** The settlement statement uses the per-lease method as required by the PSA, yielding Buyer's share of $12,941.95. The $0.01 difference is allocated to Buyer, consistent with the PSA's rounding rule (Section 7.2(c): "any remaining balance allocated to Buyer").

### B-6. No brokerage commission
Per PSA Section 15.7, no broker, finder, or real estate agent was involved and no commission is due. **Assumption:** No brokerage commission line item appears on the settlement statement. Each party indemnifies the other against any brokerage claim.

### B-7. No insurance proration
The PSA does not provide for proration of property/casualty insurance. The Seller's property insurance terminates at closing; the Buyer is required to obtain a new policy (Lender Commitment, Section 5.5: minimum $3,900,000 replacement cost, Tidewater Savings Bank named as mortgagee/loss payee, carrier rated A- or better). **Assumption:** No insurance premium or proration appears on the settlement statement. Confirm Buyer's evidence of insurance is delivered at closing as a loan condition.

### B-8. Survey, environmental, and SNDA costs not specified
The title commitment (Requirement 12) requires an ALTA/NSPS survey; the lender (Section 5.4) requires a survey and flood determination; the lender (Section 5.6) requires a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment; and the lender (Section 5.7) requires a Subordination, Non-Disturbance, and Attornment Agreement (SNDA) with Coastal Provisions Market LLC. None of these costs is stated in the PSA, title commitment, or lender commitment as a closing-table charge. **Assumption:** These items are either already completed or paid outside of closing (POC) by Buyer. No line item appears for them. Confirm with Buyer's counsel whether any survey, environmental, or SNDA fees are due at closing.

### B-9. Assignment of Leases and Rents to Lender — possible additional recording fee
The PSA (Section 6.4) lists three Buyer recording fees totaling $339.00: deed ($113), mortgage ($113), and assignment of leases ($113). The lender's recording instructions (Commitment Section 6.4) require recording of four instruments, including an "Assignment of Leases and Rents from Meridian Cove Properties LLC to Tidewater Savings Bank" (item 4). It is unclear whether the $113 "Assignment of Leases" recording fee in the PSA covers the Seller-to-Buyer assignment, the Buyer-to-Lender assignment, or both. **Assumption:** The settlement statement includes one $113.00 assignment-of-leases recording fee (Line 800). If a separate recording fee is required for the Assignment of Leases and Rents to the Lender, an additional $113.00 Buyer charge must be added. Confirm with the Closing Agent and Lender.

### B-10. Utility deposits retained by Seller
Per PSA Section 7.5(d), utility deposits held by utility providers in Seller's name are retained by Seller unless the parties otherwise agree. **Assumption:** No utility deposit transfer or credit appears on the settlement statement. Buyer is responsible for establishing new utility accounts as of the Closing Date.

---

## C. ITEMS REQUIRING CONFIRMATION OR POST-CLOSING ACTION

### C-1. Lien payoff good-through dates — closing must occur by July 20, 2025
The Harborstone first mortgage ($687,412.33) and HELOC ($148,219.56) payoffs are good through July 20, 2025. The municipal tax payoff is also valid through July 20, 2025. The scheduled closing date (July 15, 2025) falls within all good-through periods, so no per diem adjustments are required. **If closing is delayed beyond July 20, 2025:** updated payoff statements must be obtained and per diem interest added — 1st Mortgage: $112.67/day; HELOC: ~$27.93/day (variable, subject to Prime Rate change); delinquent tax: $13.12/day. The Northbridge mechanic's lien settlement ($31,000.00) is contingent on closing by August 31, 2025, after which Northbridge may withdraw the settlement and pursue the full $37,500.00 lien.

### C-2. CT DRS estate tax lien release — recording required
No monetary payoff is due ($0.00). The estate is below the Connecticut estate tax threshold. A release (CT DRS Release Certificate No. ETL-2025-08834) has been obtained and must be recorded in the Bridgeport Land Records at Seller's expense ($73.00 recording fee included in Line 511). The title company (Requirement 7) and lender (Section 5.2(d)) both require this recording as a condition of insuring/funding. Confirm the release is delivered in recordable form and recorded.

### C-3. Fiduciary certificate recency
The Fiduciary's Certificate is dated June 25, 2025. The title commitment (Requirement 2) requires it dated no more than 60 days prior to closing; the lender (Section 5.12) requires it dated no earlier than 60 days prior to closing. July 15, 2025 is 20 days after June 25, 2025 — both requirements are satisfied. No action required, but confirm a certified copy is delivered and recorded.

### C-4. Probate Court Certificate — $150.00 Seller expense
The $150.00 Probate Court Certificate fee is a Seller expense (PSA Sections 6.2(e), 6.4). The fiduciary certificate document confirms the $150.00 certification fee has been paid. Reflected as Line 514 (debit to Seller).

### C-5. Management agreement termination — $4,500.00 Seller expense
The $4,500.00 termination fee is owed to Bayshore Management Co. per Section 11.2 of the management agreement (terminated effective July 15, 2025 per notice dated June 13, 2025), payable from sale proceeds at closing. Reflected as Line 515 (debit to Seller). Bayshore is directed to transfer all security deposits (with accrued residential interest) and deliver all records/keys to Buyer or the Closing Agent on or before the Closing Date.

### C-6. Repair escrow — separate agreement; lender is interested party
The $45,000.00 Repair Escrow (Line 516) is governed by a separate Repair Escrow Agreement to be executed at closing. The lender (Commitment Section 5.13) requires that (a) the Repair Escrow Agreement be provided for lender review prior to closing, (b) Tidewater Savings Bank be named as an interested party, and (c) disbursements occur only with the lender's prior written consent after satisfactory evidence of completed work. The pre-closing inspection report (July 14, 2025) confirms the roof deficiencies remain unrepaired and recommends Buyer obtain competitive bids promptly after closing. Confirm the Repair Escrow Agreement is executed and the lender's requirements are satisfied.

### C-7. Unit 2E delinquent rent — excluded; Buyer assumes collection right
Unit 2E's July 2025 rent ($1,600.00) is delinquent and excluded from the rent proration per PSA Section 7.3. Buyer assumes the right (but not the obligation) to collect delinquent rents post-closing. Any amounts collected by Buyer are first applied to current rents owed to Buyer; excess is remitted to Seller within 30 days (Section 7.3(b)). Seller may not commence or continue eviction/collection proceedings after closing without Buyer's consent (Section 7.3(c)).

### C-8. Month-to-month holdover tenancies assigned to Buyer
Six residential leases have expired and are now month-to-month holdover tenancies: Units 2B (expired 2/28/2025), 2C (expired 5/31/2025), 2E (expired 3/31/2025), 3B (expired 1/31/2025), 3D (expired 6/30/2025), and 3F (expired 4/30/2025). These are assigned to Buyer subject to the Permitted Exceptions (PSA Section 5.2(b)). Buyer assumes all landlord obligations for these holdover tenancies, including any risk of termination on proper notice. Buyer should review holdover provisions and consider lease renewals post-closing.

### C-9. Commercial lease — SNDA and assignment required
The Commercial Lease with Coastal Provisions Market LLC (10-year term, 1/1/2021–12/31/2030; monthly base rent $7,200; security deposit $14,400; NNN) is assigned to Buyer at closing. The lender requires an SNDA among the lender, Buyer, and Coastal Provisions Market LLC prior to or at closing (Commitment Section 5.7). The lease contains a 3% annual rent escalation (next increase 1/1/2026), two 5-year renewal options, and a right of first refusal on adjacent space. Confirm the SNDA is executed and the assignment is delivered in recordable form.

### C-10. Tenant notification within 15 days
Per PSA Section 11.3, Buyer must send written notice to all tenants within 15 days after closing advising of the change in ownership, new rent payment instructions, and the new holder of security deposits, in compliance with applicable Connecticut law.

### C-11. Personal guaranty required by lender
The lender (Commitment Section 5.10) requires Elaine R. Matsuda to execute an unconditional personal guaranty of payment and performance. This is a loan condition, not a settlement-statement item, but is noted for completeness. Confirm the guaranty is executed at closing.

### C-12. Closing protection letter and gap indemnity
The title commitment (Requirement 14) requires issuance of a closing protection letter in favor of Buyer and the Lender, and execution of a gap indemnity agreement covering the period from the title search effective date (June 16, 2025) through recording. Confirm these are delivered. No cost is stated in the closing file.

### C-13. Post-closing recording sequence
Per the lender's instructions (Commitment Section 6.4), documents must be recorded in the Bridgeport Land Records in this order: (1) releases/satisfactions of all existing liens; (2) Warranty Deed (Estate to Buyer); (3) Mortgage (Buyer to Lender); (4) Assignment of Leases and Rents (Buyer to Lender). This sequence preserves the lender's first-priority lien position. The Closing Agent must confirm releases are recorded or submitted for recording immediately following closing, with written confirmation to the lender within 60 days.

### C-14. Reproration right survives 90 days
All proration and adjustment obligations survive closing for 90 days (PSA Section 15.1) for purposes of reproration and final settlement (PSA Section 7.7). Any amounts owed upon reproration must be paid within 10 business days of demand with supporting documentation. Estimated items subject to reproration: FY 2025–2026 property taxes (B-4), and any utility charges if final readings were unavailable.

---

## SUMMARY OF FINANCIAL IMPACT OF UNCONFIRMED ITEMS

| Item | Line(s) | Current Treatment | Potential Adjustment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Earnest money interest | 200a / (Seller) | $0.00 | Credit to Buyer / Debit to Seller (reduces Buyer cash & Seller proceeds equally) |
| Security deposit interest | 203a / 507a | $0.00 | Credit to Buyer / Debit to Seller (reduces Buyer cash & Seller proceeds equally) |
| Conveyance tax (actual vs. est.) | 512 / 900 | $22,375.00 each | Adjusted equally on both sides; net reconciliation impact $0.00 |
| FY 2025–26 tax (actual vs. est.) | 205 / 509 | $2,012.93 Seller | Reprorated within 90 days post-closing |
| Assignment-to-Lender recording fee | 800 | $0.00 (not included) | Possible additional $113.00 Buyer charge |
| Utility bill paid at closing vs. credited | 204 / 508 | Credit to Buyer $1,847.60 | If paid directly, remove Buyer credit; add to Seller disbursement |

> **Net effect on reconciliation:** Items that adjust both Buyer and Seller equally (earnest money interest, security deposit interest, conveyance tax) have zero net impact on the reconciliation. Items that affect only one side (assignment-to-lender recording fee, utility treatment) require a corresponding adjustment to maintain the reconciliation. The settlement statement as prepared reconciles to $0.00 with the assumptions stated above.

result:
# SETTLEMENT STATEMENT --- NOTES MEMO

## Discrepancies, Assumptions, and Items Requiring Confirmation

**Transaction:** Sale of 4280 Harborview Boulevard, Bridgeport, CT 06604 **Closing Date:** July 15, 2025 **Seller:** Estate of Gerald T. Whitford (Claudia Whitford-Barnes, Executrix) **Buyer:** Meridian Cove Properties LLC (Elaine R. Matsuda, Manager) **Prepared by:** Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC, Closing Agent

**Re:** Settlement Statement dated July 15, 2025

**Purpose.** This memorandum accompanies the settlement statement prepared for the above-referenced closing. It identifies (A) discrepancies among the closing documents, (B) assumptions made in preparing the settlement statement where the record is silent or ambiguous, and (C) items requiring confirmation or post-closing action. Each item is keyed to the source document and, where applicable, to the affected settlement-statement line. The settlement statement should be read together with this memo.

## A. DISCREPANCIES AMONG CLOSING DOCUMENTS

### A-1. Title commitment number mismatch

The Purchase & Sale Agreement (Section 5.1) references the title commitment as "Commitment No. PCT-2025-08814," dated April 30, 2025. The actual title commitment delivered by Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC bears Commitment No. **TC-2025-07182**, effective June 18, 2025. The commitment number and effective date in the PSA do not match the commitment in the closing file. The settlement statement uses the actual commitment (TC-2025-07182). **Action:** Confirm with the Title Company that TC-2025-07182 is the operative commitment and that no separate commitment PCT-2025-08814 exists or was superseded.

### A-2. Title insurer name inconsistency within the title commitment

The title commitment header states it is issued "on behalf of **Continental Fiducial Title Insurance Company**," but the body and Conditions & Stipulations refer to "**Continental Hartleigh Title Insurance Company**" as "the Company." The countersignature block again references "Continental Fiducial Title Insurance Company." It is unclear whether the underwriter is Continental Fiducity or Continental Hartleigh. **Action:** Confirm the correct legal name of the title insurer for the owner's and lender's policies; correct the commitment and policy forms as needed.

### A-3. Recording references for liens differ between title commitment and payoff letters

The recording references (volume/page) for the existing liens are inconsistent between the title commitment (Schedule B-I) and the lien payoff letter compilation prepared by Seller's counsel:

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Lien                          Title Commitment Reference   Payoff Letter / Summary Reference
  ----------------------------- ---------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Harborstone 1st Mortgage      Vol. 1087, Pg. 445           Vol. 312, Pg. 88 *(this is the property plat/deed reference, not the mortgage)*

  Harborstone HELOC             Vol. 1204, Pg. 218           Vol. 487, Pg. 112

  Northbridge Mechanic's Lien   Vol. 1398, Pg. 77            Vol. 1064, Pg. 517

  CT DRS Estate Tax Lien        *(not specified)*            Vol. 1087, Pg. 234
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The payoff letter for the first mortgage incorrectly cites "Volume 312, Page 88" as the mortgage recording reference --- that volume/page is the Bridgeport Harbor Redevelopment Plat (the property's legal description), not the mortgage recording. **Action:** Reconcile the correct recording references with the Bridgeport Land Records before recording releases; ensure each release instrument cites the correct volume/page of the lien it discharges. The settlement statement's payoff amounts are unaffected, but the release instruments must reference the correct recordings.

### A-4. Utility bill treatment --- PSA vs. municipal lien certificate

The PSA (Section 7.5(a)) directs that where a utility billing period ends on or before the Closing Date, "the full unpaid balance shall be credited to Buyer on the settlement statement." The water/sewer billing period (May 15 -- July 14, 2025) ends July 14, one day before closing, so the entire \$1,847.60 is Seller's responsibility. However, both the utility account statement and the municipal tax status certificate (No. TLC-2025-04892) state that this balance "must be satisfied at or before closing," even though the bill is not due until August 1, 2025. The settlement statement follows the PSA (debit Seller / credit Buyer, Lines 508/204). **Action:** The Closing Agent should confirm whether the WPCA bill is paid directly from Seller's proceeds at closing (satisfying the municipal lien certificate) or credited to Buyer for post-closing payment. If paid directly from Seller's proceeds, the credit to Buyer (Line 204) should be removed and Buyer's cash adjusted upward by \$1,847.60; the reconciliation remains balanced either way.

### A-5. First mortgage per diem interest rate appears inconsistent with stated rate

The first mortgage payoff statement shows accrued interest of \$73.07/day for the 12-day period (consistent with 3.875% on the \~\$685,921 balance: \$685,921 × 3.875% ÷ 365 ≈ \$72.83/day). However, the stated per diem after the good-through date is **\$112.67/day**, which implies an effective rate of approximately 5.99% --- inconsistent with the stated 3.875% fixed rate. The payoff letter attributes this to "the higher daily interest accrual rate that applies to the declining principal balance," which does not explain a higher (not lower) per diem. **Impact on settlement:** None, because closing (July 15) falls within the good-through period (through July 20) and no per diem adjustment is required. **Action:** If closing is delayed beyond July 20, 2025, obtain a corrected/updated payoff statement and verify the per diem before disbursing additional interest.

### A-6. Address inconsistencies for Closing Agent and Buyer's counsel

The closing documents contain multiple, inconsistent addresses for the same parties:

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Party                                        PSA / Title Commitment                              Lien Payoff Letter                                   Tax Lien Certificate
  -------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------
  Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC                88 Field Point Road, Greenwich, CT 06830            3100 Main Street, Suite 204, Bridgeport, CT 06604    1200 Post Road East, Suite 214, Westport, CT 06880

  Ridgeline Law Group PLLC (Buyer's Counsel)   915 Chapel Street, Suite 400, New Haven, CT 06510   55 Railroad Avenue, Suite 300, Greenwich, CT 06830   915 Chapel Street, Suite 400, New Haven, CT 06510
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Action:** Confirm the correct mailing/service addresses for the Closing Agent and Buyer's counsel for purposes of wire instructions, document delivery, and post-closing correspondence. Wire instructions must be verified telephonically with a known contact regardless of the address listed.

### A-7. Escrow shortage included in first mortgage payoff

The Harborstone first mortgage payoff of \$687,412.33 includes a \$539.00 "Escrow Shortage / Deficiency" and a \$75.00 "Release / Reconveyance Preparation Fee" in addition to the unpaid principal balance (\$685,921.44) and accrued interest (\$876.89). These are included in the total payoff disbursed from Seller's proceeds (Line 501). The \$75.00 release preparation fee is distinct from the \$73.00 recording fee for the release (Line 511). **Note:** Confirm with Seller's counsel that the escrow shortage is properly the Estate's obligation and that no escrow refund will be due to the Estate post-payoff (the payoff letter states overpayments are refunded to the Estate).

## B. ASSUMPTIONS MADE IN PREPARING THE SETTLEMENT STATEMENT

### B-1. Accrued interest on earnest money --- not quantified (Lines 200a / 200a)

The PSA (Sections 3.2(d), 3.2(e)) requires the Earnest Money (\$195,000.00) to be held in an interest-bearing account, with all accrued interest credited to Buyer at closing and applied against the Purchase Price. No document in the closing file states the interest amount earned on the First Deposit (held since April 24, 2025) or the Second Deposit (held since May 22, 2025). **Assumption:** The settlement statement credits \$195,000.00 as earnest money with \$0.00 accrued interest (Line 200a). When the Escrow Agent confirms the accrued interest, it shall be added as a credit to Buyer (Line 200a) and a corresponding debit to Seller, reducing Buyer's cash at closing and Seller's net proceeds by equal amounts.

### B-2. Accrued interest on residential security deposits --- not quantified (Lines 203a / 507a)

The PSA (Section 7.4(a)) requires transfer of security deposits "together with any interest accrued thereon as required by applicable law." Connecticut General Statutes § 47a-21 requires interest on residential security deposits. The rent roll/security deposit schedule confirms residential deposits are held in an interest-bearing escrow account at Harborstone Federal Credit Union, but states no interest amount. The commercial deposit (\$14,400.00) is held in a non-interest-bearing account per the management agreement (Section 6.2) and the commercial lease, so no interest accrues on it. **Assumption:** The settlement statement credits \$32,400.00 in deposit principal only (Line 203), with \$0.00 interest (Line 203a). When the Property Manager (Bayshore Management Co.) confirms the accrued interest on the 11 residential deposits, it shall be added as a credit to Buyer (Line 203a) and debit to Seller (Line 507a).

### B-3. Connecticut conveyance tax --- estimated (Lines 512 / 900)

The total conveyance tax of \$44,750.00 (\$22,375.00 per party) is an estimate taken from the title commitment (Schedule C), stated to be "calculated using the tiered rate structure applicable to the City of Bridgeport." The PSA (Article 12) provides that the tax be "computed at the rate or rates applicable to the municipality" and split 50/50. The actual amount will be determined upon preparation and filing of Form OP-236 by the Closing Agent. **Assumption:** The settlement statement uses the title commitment's estimate. If the actual tax differs, each party's 50% share is adjusted equally; the net effect on the reconciliation is zero (both sides move by the same amount).

### B-4. FY 2025--2026 property tax --- estimated using current year (Lines 205 / 509)

result:
The municipal tax status certificate (Section 3) confirms that tax bills for the fiscal year commencing July 1, 2025 have not been issued and the new mill rate has not been set. Per PSA Section 7.1(b), the parties agreed to prorate using the current year's tax of \$52,480.00 as the estimate. **Assumption:** The settlement statement uses \$52,480.00 ÷ 365 = \$143.7808/day, with Seller charged for 14 days (\$2,012.93) and Buyer responsible for 351 days (\$50,467.07). The parties shall reprorate within 90 days after closing when the actual bill is issued (PSA Section 7.7).

### B-5. Rent proration methodology --- per-lease aggregation (Lines 202 / 506)

The PSA (Section 7.2(d)) requires rents to be "calculated separately for each lease and then aggregated into a single net proration amount." When calculated per-lease and aggregated, Seller's share is \$10,658.05 and Buyer's share is \$12,941.95. When calculated on the aggregate collected rent (\$23,600.00 × 14/31 and × 17/31), Seller's share would be \$10,658.06 and Buyer's share \$12,941.94 --- a \$0.01 difference due to rounding. **Assumption:** The settlement statement uses the per-lease method as required by the PSA, yielding Buyer's share of \$12,941.95. The \$0.01 difference is allocated to Buyer, consistent with the PSA's rounding rule (Section 7.2(c): "any remaining balance allocated to Buyer").

### B-6. No brokerage commission

Per PSA Section 15.7, no broker, finder, or real estate agent was involved and no commission is due. **Assumption:** No brokerage commission line item appears on the settlement statement. Each party indemnifies the other against any brokerage claim.

### B-7. No insurance proration

The PSA does not provide for proration of property/casualty insurance. The Seller's property insurance terminates at closing; the Buyer is required to obtain a new policy (Lender Commitment, Section 5.5: minimum \$3,900,000 replacement cost, Tidewater Savings Bank named as mortgagee/loss payee, carrier rated A- or better). **Assumption:** No insurance premium or proration appears on the settlement statement. Confirm Buyer's evidence of insurance is delivered at closing as a loan condition.

### B-8. Survey, environmental, and SNDA costs not specified

The title commitment (Requirement 12) requires an ALTA/NSPS survey; the lender (Section 5.4) requires a survey and flood determination; the lender (Section 5.6) requires a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment; and the lender (Section 5.7) requires a Subordination, Non-Disturbance, and Attornment Agreement (SNDA) with Coastal Provisions Market LLC. None of these costs is stated in the PSA, title commitment, or lender commitment as a closing-table charge. **Assumption:** These items are either already completed or paid outside of closing (POC) by Buyer. No line item appears for them. Confirm with Buyer's counsel whether any survey, environmental, or SNDA fees are due at closing.

### B-9. Assignment of Leases and Rents to Lender --- possible additional recording fee

The PSA (Section 6.4) lists three Buyer recording fees totaling \$339.00: deed (\$113), mortgage (\$113), and assignment of leases (\$113). The lender's recording instructions (Commitment Section 6.4) require recording of four instruments, including an "Assignment of Leases and Rents from Meridian Cove Properties LLC to Tidewater Savings Bank" (item 4). It is unclear whether the \$113 "Assignment of Leases" recording fee in the PSA covers the Seller-to-Buyer assignment, the Buyer-to-Lender assignment, or both. **Assumption:** The settlement statement includes one \$113.00 assignment-of-leases recording fee (Line 800). If a separate recording fee is required for the Assignment of Leases and Rents to the Lender, an additional \$113.00 Buyer charge must be added. Confirm with the Closing Agent and Lender.

### B-10. Utility deposits retained by Seller

Per PSA Section 7.5(d), utility deposits held by utility providers in Seller's name are retained by Seller unless the parties otherwise agree. **Assumption:** No utility deposit transfer or credit appears on the settlement statement. Buyer is responsible for establishing new utility accounts as of the Closing Date.

## C. ITEMS REQUIRING CONFIRMATION OR POST-CLOSING ACTION

### C-1. Lien payoff good-through dates --- closing must occur by July 20, 2025

The Harborstone first mortgage (\$687,412.33) and HELOC (\$148,219.56) payoffs are good through July 20, 2025. The municipal tax payoff is also valid through July 20, 2025. The scheduled closing date (July 15, 2025) falls within all good-through periods, so no per diem adjustments are required. **If closing is delayed beyond July 20, 2025:** updated payoff statements must be obtained and per diem interest added --- 1st Mortgage: \$112.67/day; HELOC: \~\$27.93/day (variable, subject to Prime Rate change); delinquent tax: \$13.12/day. The Northbridge mechanic's lien settlement (\$31,000.00) is contingent on closing by August 31, 2025, after which Northbridge may withdraw the settlement and pursue the full \$37,500.00 lien.

### C-2. CT DRS estate tax lien release --- recording required

No monetary payoff is due (\$0.00). The estate is below the Connecticut estate tax threshold. A release (CT DRS Release Certificate No. ETL-2025-08834) has been obtained and must be recorded in the Bridgeport Land Records at Seller's expense (\$73.00 recording fee included in Line 511). The title company (Requirement 7) and lender (Section 5.2(d)) both require this recording as a condition of insuring/funding. Confirm the release is delivered in recordable form and recorded.

### C-3. Fiduciary certificate recency

The Fiduciary's Certificate is dated June 25, 2025. The title commitment (Requirement 2) requires it dated no more than 60 days prior to closing; the lender (Section 5.12) requires it dated no earlier than 60 days prior to closing. July 15, 2025 is 20 days after June 25, 2025 --- both requirements are satisfied. No action required, but confirm a certified copy is delivered and recorded.

### C-4. Probate Court Certificate --- \$150.00 Seller expense

The \$150.00 Probate Court Certificate fee is a Seller expense (PSA Sections 6.2(e), 6.4). The fiduciary certificate document confirms the \$150.00 certification fee has been paid. Reflected as Line 514 (debit to Seller).

### C-5. Management agreement termination --- \$4,500.00 Seller expense

The \$4,500.00 termination fee is owed to Bayshore Management Co. per Section 11.2 of the management agreement (terminated effective July 15, 2025 per notice dated June 13, 2025), payable from sale proceeds at closing. Reflected as Line 515 (debit to Seller). Bayshore is directed to transfer all security deposits (with accrued residential interest) and deliver all records/keys to Buyer or the Closing Agent on or before the Closing Date.

### C-6. Repair escrow --- separate agreement; lender is interested party

The \$45,000.00 Repair Escrow (Line 516) is governed by a separate Repair Escrow Agreement to be executed at closing. The lender (Commitment Section 5.13) requires that (a) the Repair Escrow Agreement be provided for lender review prior to closing, (b) Tidewater Savings Bank be named as an interested party, and (c) disbursements occur only with the lender's prior written consent after satisfactory evidence of completed work. The pre-closing inspection report (July 14, 2025) confirms the roof deficiencies remain unrepaired and recommends Buyer obtain competitive bids promptly after closing. Confirm the Repair Escrow Agreement is executed and the lender's requirements are satisfied.

### C-7. Unit 2E delinquent rent --- excluded; Buyer assumes collection right

Unit 2E's July 2025 rent (\$1,600.00) is delinquent and excluded from the rent proration per PSA Section 7.3. Buyer assumes the right (but not the obligation) to collect delinquent rents post-closing. Any amounts collected by Buyer are first applied to current rents owed to Buyer; excess is remitted to Seller within 30 days (Section 7.3(b)). Seller may not commence or continue eviction/collection proceedings after closing without Buyer's consent (Section 7.3(c)).

### C-8. Month-to-month holdover tenancies assigned to Buyer

Six residential leases have expired and are now month-to-month holdover tenancies: Units 2B (expired 2/28/2025), 2C (expired 5/31/2025), 2E (expired 3/31/2025), 3B (expired 1/31/2025), 3D (expired 6/30/2025), and 3F (expired 4/30/2025). These are assigned to Buyer subject to the Permitted Exceptions (PSA Section 5.2(b)). Buyer assumes all landlord obligations for these holdover tenancies, including any risk of termination on proper notice. Buyer should review holdover provisions and consider lease renewals post-closing.

### C-9. Commercial lease --- SNDA and assignment required

The Commercial Lease with Coastal Provisions Market LLC (10-year term, 1/1/2021--12/31/2030; monthly base rent \$7,200; security deposit \$14,400; NNN) is assigned to Buyer at closing. The lender requires an SNDA among the lender, Buyer, and Coastal Provisions Market LLC prior to or at closing (Commitment Section 5.7). The lease contains a 3% annual rent escalation (next increase 1/1/2026), two 5-year renewal options, and a right of first refusal on adjacent space. Confirm the SNDA is executed and the assignment is delivered in recordable form.

### C-10. Tenant notification within 15 days

Per PSA Section 11.3, Buyer must send written notice to all tenants within 15 days after closing advising of the change in ownership, new rent payment instructions, and the new holder of security deposits, in compliance with applicable Connecticut law.

### C-11. Personal guaranty required by lender

The lender (Commitment Section 5.10) requires Elaine R. Matsuda to execute an unconditional personal guaranty of payment and performance. This is a loan condition, not a settlement-statement item, but is noted for completeness. Confirm the guaranty is executed at closing.

### C-12. Closing protection letter and gap indemnity

The title commitment (Requirement 14) requires issuance of a closing protection letter in favor of Buyer and the Lender, and execution of a gap indemnity agreement covering the period from the title search effective date (June 16, 2025) through recording. Confirm these are delivered. No cost is stated in the closing file.

### C-13. Post-closing recording sequence

Per the lender's instructions (Commitment Section 6.4), documents must be recorded in the Bridgeport Land Records in this order: (1) releases/satisfactions of all existing liens; (2) Warranty Deed (Estate to Buyer); (3) Mortgage (Buyer to Lender); (4) Assignment of Leases and Rents (Buyer to Lender). This sequence preserves the lender's first-priority lien position. The Closing Agent must confirm releases are recorded or submitted for recording immediately following closing, with written confirmation to the lender within 60 days.

I have completed the task. Here is a summary of what was delivered:

## Deliverables

Both files are in the output directory and pass schema validation:

### `settlement-statement.docx`
A complete commercial settlement statement (HUD-1 style) covering the closing of 4280 Harborview Boulevard, Bridgeport, CT (Estate of Gerald T. Whitford → Meridian Cove Properties LLC, July 15, 2025):

- **Buyer's side (Sections 100–300):** Gross due $3,967,767.00 → credits $2,884,202.48 → **cash from Buyer $1,083,564.52**
- **Seller's side (Sections 400–600):** Gross due $3,900,693.00 → reductions $1,036,029.17 → **net proceeds to Seller $2,864,663.83**
- **Full settlement charges breakdown** (title, recording, conveyance tax, loan, attorney/other) plus POC disclosure for the $4,500 appraisal fee
- **Five detailed proration schedules:** (A) real property taxes — delinquent $28,602.80 from Seller proceeds + FY25–26 estimate split 14/351 days; (B) rents — per-lease, 14/17 days, $12,941.95 to Buyer, with Unit 2E (delinquent) and Unit 3C (vacant) excluded; (C) security deposits $32,400.00; (D) water/sewer $1,847.60; (E) heating oil $693.00
- **Reconciliation summary:** Funds in = Funds out = **$3,918,564.52, difference $0.00 — RECONCILED**, plus equity verification and wire disbursement summary

### `settlement-statement-notes.docx`
A notes memo flagging **7 discrepancies** (title commitment number mismatch, insurer name inconsistency, conflicting lien recording references, utility-bill treatment conflict, per diem rate inconsistency, party address conflicts, escrow shortage in payoff), **10 assumptions** (unquantified earnest-money and security-deposit interest, estimated conveyance tax and FY25–26 tax, per-lease rent methodology, and more), and **14 items requiring confirmation/post-closing action** — with a summary table of the financial impact of each unconfirmed item.

All figures were verified programmatically and reconcile exactly. Every source document in the closing file was read and mined for issues.

Before you finish, run a mandatory COMPLETENESS AUDIT of your deliverable(s) against the source record. Do not assume your first draft covered everything — the most common failure on this kind of task is a deliverable that is polished but omits distinct, individually-graded points the record supports.

Do this now, as real tool calls (not in your head):
1. RE-LIST every distinct issue/requirement the source documents raise. Re-read the key source files and enumerate each separate problem, gap, discrepancy, nonconformance, or required element — one per item, not bundled. Include the ones you already wrote about AND any you have not yet addressed.
2. CROSS-CHECK your current deliverable against that list. For each item, is it actually present and explicitly addressed in the output file? Open the output file and verify by content, not memory. List every item that is missing, vague, bundled into another point, or only implied.
3. CHECK EACH REQUIRED OUTPUT / DIMENSION: confirm every deliverable the task asked for exists, and that any required comparison, matrix, or dimension (e.g. every jurisdiction, every party, every provision, every document in the file) is covered — do not drop a category because it seemed minor.
4. FILL THE GAPS: use edit/write to add every missing item with its own explicit treatment, drawing the specific facts from the source documents (cite the source). Do not fabricate issues the record does not support; do add the ones it does that you skipped.
5. Re-validate any binary deliverable per its skill manual, then finish.

If a thorough re-read finds nothing missing, say so briefly and finish. Otherwise, make the additions now.

**MERIDIAN COVE PROPERTIES LLC**, a Connecticut limited liability company formed on January 8, 2024, having a principal office c/o Ridgeline Law Group PLLC, 915 Chapel Street, Suite 400, New Haven, CT 06510 (the \"Buyer\"), managed by Elaine R. Matsuda.

**[RECITALS]{.underline}**

**A.** Seller is the owner of certain real property commonly known as 4280 Harborview Boulevard, Bridgeport, CT 06604, more particularly described in Exhibit A attached hereto and made a part hereof (the \"Property\").

**B.** The Property consists of a mixed-use building containing approximately 18,400 square feet of total building area, situated on approximately 0.38 acres of land, comprising a ground-floor commercial space of approximately 3,200 square feet and twelve (12) residential apartment units (Units 2A through 2F on the second floor and Units 3A through 3F on the third floor). The Property is located within the MU-2 (Mixed-Use Transit-Oriented District) zoning classification of the City of Bridgeport. The building was originally constructed in 1998 and underwent a partial renovation in 2019.

**C.** The Property is currently subject to a commercial lease with Coastal Provisions Market LLC (the \"Commercial Tenant\") for the ground-floor commercial space, and various residential leases with tenants occupying the residential units on the second and third floors. The Property is presently managed by Bayshore Management Co. (the \"Property Manager\") under a management agreement with the Estate.

**D.** Seller desires to sell, and Buyer desires to purchase, the Property on the terms and conditions set forth herein.

**E.** Seller has the authority to convey the Property pursuant to the fiduciary powers granted under the Last Will and Testament of Gerald T. Whitford and the orders of the Fairfield County Probate Court, Docket No. 2024-PR-04417.

**NOW, THEREFORE**, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements set forth herein, and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the parties agree as follows:

Right-click to update Table of Contents

**[ARTICLE 1 --- DEFINITIONS]{.underline}**

As used in this Agreement, the following terms shall have the meanings set forth below:

**\"Agreement\"** means this Purchase and Sale Agreement, together with all exhibits attached hereto and incorporated herein by reference, as the same may be amended from time to time in accordance with Section 15.4.

**\"Buyer\"** means Meridian Cove Properties LLC, a Connecticut limited liability company.

**\"Buyer\'s Counsel\"** means Ridgeline Law Group PLLC, 915 Chapel Street, Suite 400, New Haven, CT 06510, Attn: Nora F. Kapadia, Esq.

**\"Closing\"** means the consummation of the transaction contemplated by this Agreement, including the delivery of the Deed and the payment of the Purchase Price.

**\"Closing Date\"** means July 15, 2025, or such other date as the parties may mutually agree in writing.

**\"Commercial Lease\"** means that certain lease agreement between Seller (as successor in interest to Gerald T. Whitford) and Coastal Provisions Market LLC, dated on or about January 1, 2021, for a term of ten (10) years, commencing January 1, 2021, and expiring December 31, 2030, for the ground-floor commercial space at the Property.

**\"Commercial Tenant\"** means Coastal Provisions Market LLC, a Connecticut limited liability company operated by Ryan and Sara Mulgrew.

**\"Deed\"** means the Executor\'s Warranty Deed to be delivered by Seller to Buyer at Closing, conveying the Property to Buyer.

**\"Due Diligence Period\"** means the thirty (30)-day period commencing on the Effective Date and expiring on May 22, 2025.

**\"Earnest Money\"** means the deposits described in Section 3.2 of this Agreement.

**\"Escrow Agent\"** means Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC, 88 Field Point Road, Greenwich, CT 06830, Attn: Lorraine M. Grasso, Closing Officer.

**\"Lender\"** means Tidewater Savings Bank, 3100 Post Road, Fairfield, CT 06824.

**\"Permitted Exceptions\"** means those exceptions to title described in Section 5.2 of this Agreement.

**\"Property\"** means the real property described in Exhibit A attached hereto, together with all buildings, improvements, appurtenances, fixtures, and personal property located thereon.

**\"Property Manager\"** means Bayshore Management Co., Attn: Tomás Herrera, Operations Director.

**\"Purchase Price\"** means Three Million Nine Hundred Thousand and 00/100 Dollars (\$3,900,000.00).

**\"Repair Escrow\"** means the escrow described in Section 8.4 of this Agreement.

**\"Residential Leases\"** means, collectively, the lease agreements for Units 2A, 2B, 2C, 2D, 2E, 2F, 3A, 3B, 3C, 3D, 3E, and 3F on the second and third floors of the Property, as more particularly identified on the rent roll attached as Exhibit B.

**\"Seller\"** means the Estate of Gerald T. Whitford, acting by and through Claudia Whitford-Barnes, as Executrix.

**\"Seller\'s Counsel\"** means Ashford, Clement & Paige LLP, 200 Atlantic Street, 14th Floor, Stamford, CT 06901, Attn: David H. Clement, Esq.

**\"Title Company\"** means Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC, 88 Field Point Road, Greenwich, CT 06830.

**[ARTICLE 2 --- SALE AND PURCHASE]{.underline}**

**Section 2.1 --- Agreement to Sell and Purchase**

Subject to the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement, Seller agrees to sell, transfer, and convey to Buyer, and Buyer agrees to purchase from Seller, the Property for the Purchase Price. The obligations of each party to consummate the transaction contemplated herein are subject to the satisfaction or waiver of the conditions set forth in Article 8 of this Agreement.

**Section 2.2 --- Property Description**

The Property is located at 4280 Harborview Boulevard, Bridgeport, CT 06604, County of Fairfield, State of Connecticut. The Property is legally described as Lot 17, Block 42, Bridgeport Harbor Redevelopment Plat, as recorded in Volume 312, Page 88 of the Bridgeport Land Records. The sale shall include all buildings, improvements, fixtures, and appurtenances located on, attached to, or used in connection with the Property. The Property is more fully described in Exhibit A attached hereto and incorporated herein by reference.

**Section 2.3 --- Included in Sale**

The sale of the Property shall include all of Seller\'s right, title, and interest in and to the following:

> \(a\) The land and all improvements thereon, including the mixed-use building and all related structures, parking areas, landscaping, and site improvements;
>
> \(b\) All fixtures and building systems, including without limitation heating, ventilation, air conditioning, plumbing, electrical, fire safety, and security systems;
>
> \(c\) The Commercial Lease and all Residential Leases, together with all security deposits held by Seller or the Property Manager thereunder;
>
> \(d\) All assignable permits, licenses, certificates of occupancy, warranties, and guaranties relating to the Property or any improvements thereon; and
>
> \(e\) The trade name \"4280 Harborview,\" if any, and any goodwill associated therewith.

**[ARTICLE 3 --- PURCHASE PRICE AND EARNEST MONEY]{.underline}**

**Section 3.1 --- Purchase Price**

The Purchase Price for the Property shall be Three Million Nine Hundred Thousand and 00/100 Dollars (\$3,900,000.00), payable as follows:

> \(a\) Earnest Money deposits as set forth in Section 3.2 of this Agreement;
>
> \(b\) Buyer\'s mortgage loan proceeds from Tidewater Savings Bank in the approximate amount of Two Million Six Hundred Forty Thousand and 00/100 Dollars (\$2,640,000.00); and
>
> \(c\) The balance of the Purchase Price, as adjusted for prorations, credits, and other adjustments set forth herein, in immediately available funds at Closing.
